summary: Gruber reflects on how SXSW Interactive has fundamentally changed as it grew from 2,500 to 25,000 attendees, transforming from a cohesive conference into a sprawling, poorly organized event. He agrees with Andy Budd's assessment that SXSW 'jumped the shark' around 2008/09 and is now an entirely different thing. While he still enjoys Austin and seeing friends, he considers the conference itself broken — with good talks scheduled in obscure locations and empty rooms hosting mediocre panels. He notes the irony that the smart move at a conference is now to skip the sessions entirely.
key_points: SXSW Interactive grew from 2,500 to 25,000 attendees, fundamentally changing its character in ways that can't be managed
The conference scheduling is broken: Matt Haughey's excellent talk drew only 30-40 people because it was placed a mile from the convention center
Most attendees now skip sessions entirely, treating SXSW as a networking and social event rather than a conference
The event may be as well-produced as possible given 25,000 people and Austin's space constraints — the problem may be unsolvable at that scale
money_quotes: You can't go from a conference of 2,500 attendees to one of 25,000 attendees without turning the event into something entirely different.
I don't see how anyone could claim that the conference now is anything but broken.
Used to be that SXSW was an interesting conference and a great weekend experience. Now it's a terrible conference and a good-but-crowded weekend experience.
There's something sad about a conference where it's now considered the smart move not to even attend.
I just don't think it's possible to program a cohesive, interesting, accessible session schedule given those constraints.
There's got to be a better way.
key_insight: Conferences that scale past a certain size inevitably sacrifice the cohesion and quality that made them worth attending in the first place, leaving only the social scene.
